candidates.comparison: exact text/token equality via check_outputs_equal (output_str_0 == output_str_1, output_ids_0 == output_ids_1) and exact logprob matching
candidates.oracle: Compares generation outputs and logprobs across different scheduling configs (async vs sync, preemption, executors, chunked prefill). Uses FP32 matmul precision, greedy decoding, flex_attention backend.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text/token equality across configs. Even with FP32 and greedy, different scheduling paths can produce legitimately different token sequences due to batch composition effects on matmul numerics.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes (e.g., matmul precision, attention kernel changes) could cause divergence between the baseline and test configs even though both produce valid outputs.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - there is no mathematical guarantee that different scheduling/executor configs produce identical token sequences. The test assumes bitwise reproducibility across code paths that differ in batch layout and execution order.

candidates.comparison: exact text/token equality via check_outputs_equal plus acceptance rate thresholds (tolerance 0.05-0.10 or >0.1)
candidates.oracle: Compares spec decode outputs across configs. Uses check_outputs_equal for exact match against baseline, and checks acceptance rates with tolerances.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text/token equality is required across configs with different scheduling, preemption, and chunked prefill settings. Acceptance rate thresholds are heuristic.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could cause token divergence between configs that use different batch sizes, attention paths, or spec decode code paths.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees spec decode with different scheduling configs produces identical output. The spec decode verification path itself depends on numeric comparison.

candidates.comparison: exact text/token equality via check_outputs_equal, acceptance rate checks
candidates.oracle: Same pattern as above - compares ngram_gpu spec decode outputs across scheduling configs using exact match.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text/token equality across scheduling configs.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could cause divergence between different batch/scheduling configurations.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees identical output across different scheduling/executor configurations.

candidates.oracle: Generates single prompt (no cascade), then 64 copies of same prompt (triggers cascade attention). Asserts all 64 outputs exactly match the single-prompt reference. Uses greedy (temp=0).
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text equality between single-prompt and batched generation. Different batch sizes take different code paths (cascade vs non-cascade attention).
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch changes to attention kernels or matmul could cause cascade attention path to produce different results from non-cascade path, even with greedy decoding.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - there is no contract guaranteeing that cascade attention produces bitwise-identical output to non-cascade attention. These are different algorithmic paths.

candidates.comparison: check_answers with accept_rate=1.0 (exact 2-char integer comparison from output text)
candidates.oracle: Generates prompts with variable assignments (x30=42, etc.), asks model to recall a specific variable value. Checks that first 2 chars of output match the expected integer. Uses accept_rate=1.0 meaning 100% must match.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact integer equality from text extraction. The oracle extracts first 2 chars of output and compares as integer. With accept_rate=1.0, ALL prompts must produce exact match.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could cause the model to recall a different value or format the answer differently, breaking the exact integer match.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - there is no contract that the model will always produce the exact expected number as its first two output characters. This depends on model behavior which is sensitive to numeric precision.
candidates.code_snippet: check_answers(indices, answer, [response.outputs[0].text for response in responses], accept_rate=1.0)

candidates.oracle: Tests KV sharing fast prefill feature with gemma-3n model. Uses prep_prompts (variable assignment recall) and check_answers requiring 100% accuracy.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact integer equality from text extraction with accept_rate=1.0.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes (especially GELU implementation, attention kernels) could affect model recall accuracy.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees model will always recall exact variable values from context. This is an emergent capability test.
candidates.code_snippet: check_answers(indices, answer, [response.outputs[0].text for response in responses], accept_rate=1.0)

candidates.oracle: Tests that bunched and spaced streaming inputs produce identical output text with temperature=0.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text equality between bunched and spaced delivery of same prompts. Different timing/batching could affect numerics.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could cause different batch compositions (bunched vs spaced arrive at different times) to produce different outputs.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees that bunched vs spaced delivery produces identical text. The timing difference affects scheduling and potentially batch composition.

candidates.comparison: exact text equality (ref_output.outputs[0].text == spec_output.outputs[0].text), matches > 90% threshold
candidates.oracle: Compares LoRA-only LLM output vs LoRA+spec-decode output. Expects 90% exact text match rate. Uses greedy decoding, fixed seed, deterministic cublas.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text equality with 90% threshold. The threshold acknowledges FP noise but the comparison itself is exact text match.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes (matmul, attention) could increase the mismatch rate beyond 10%, breaking the 90% threshold.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees spec decode produces identical text to non-spec decode. The verification path runs at different seqlen which affects matmul numerics.
candidates.code_snippet: if ref_output.outputs[0].text == spec_output.outputs[0].text: matches += 1 ... assert matches > threshold

candidates.comparison: GSM8K accuracy thresholds (0.7-0.8), exact text match >= 66%, config assertions
candidates.oracle: Tests speculators model integration. Verifies auto-detection of spec config, GSM8K accuracy, and >= 66% exact output match vs reference.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - the 66% exact text match threshold is a weak oracle. While GSM8K accuracy has margin, exact text comparison is brittle.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could reduce the exact match rate below 66%, especially since spec decode verification runs different seqlen matmuls.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees 66% exact text match between spec and non-spec decode.

candidates.comparison: exact text match > 60% (via _run_eagle_correctness), GSM8K accuracy threshold
candidates.oracle: Tests EAGLE spec decode correctness. Compares spec vs non-spec outputs, expects >60% exact match. Also checks GSM8K accuracy.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text match with 60% threshold. The threshold is generous but still relies on exact text comparison.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could reduce exact match rate below 60%.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract on exact text match rate between spec and non-spec decode.

candidates.comparison: exact text match > 60%, GSM8K accuracy thresholds (0.7-0.8)
candidates.oracle: Same pattern as test_eagle_correctness_light but with different models (Qwen3, Llama3).
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text match with 60% threshold.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could reduce match rate.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract on match rate.

candidates.comparison: exact text match > 60%, GSM8K accuracy thresholds
candidates.oracle: Same pattern as eagle_correctness_light/medium but with heavier models (Llama3.1, Llama4-Scout).
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text match with 60% threshold.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could reduce match rate.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract on match rate.

candidates.comparison: exact text match > 80% (MTP_SIMILARITY_RATE=0.8), GSM8K accuracy thresholds (0.2-0.5)
candidates.oracle: Tests MTP spec decode correctness. Compares spec vs non-spec outputs, expects >80% exact match. Also checks GSM8K accuracy.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: YES - exact text match with 80% threshold.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could reduce exact match rate below 80%.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract on exact text match rate.

candidates.comparison: exact substring check: "64" in responses[0] and "43" in responses[1]
candidates.oracle: Tests MTP spec decode with mixed batch short prefill. Sends two prompts with embedded facts (secret number 64, secret number 42+1=43). Checks that responses contain the expected numbers.
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: PARTIAL - substring check ('43' in response) is looser than exact equality but still depends on model generating the specific number.
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: YES - PyTorch numeric changes could cause the model to fail to recall/compute the correct numbers, especially with the mixed batch/short prefill path that this test is specifically designed to stress.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: YES - no contract guarantees the model will produce '43' in its output. This is an emergent capability test.
